results.possibleIdentity: Walter Wright is an interdisciplinary video artist, computer animator, and software engineer who has been a pioneer in the field of electronic arts since the late 1960s. Originally trained in architecture at the University of Manitoba and the University of Waterloo, he shifted his focus to computer graphics and electronic music around 1966. In the early 1970s, he became one of the first video animators at Computer Image Corp in New York City, where he worked with the Scanimate analog computer system to create early computer-generated visuals. He is well-known for his collaboration with Ed Emshwiller on the landmark video works 'Thermogenesis' and 'Scapemates' (1971), the latter being the first computer-animated video to be nominated for an Emmy Award. Wright's work from this era was deeply influenced by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement and the research conducted at Bell Laboratories. In 1973, he succeeded Nam June Paik as the artist-in-residence at the Experimental Television Center (ETC) in Binghamton, New York, where he pioneered live video performance using the Paik/Abe Video Synthesizer. He also served as an Associate Director at The Kitchen in New York City and collaborated with artists such as Gary Hill and Sun Ra. Later in his career, he developed his own real-time video processing systems, including the 'Video Shredder,' and co-founded the 119 Gallery (formerly 911 Gallery), one of the first digital art galleries on the web. He remains active in the Boston area as a multimedia improviser, performing with electronic instruments and custom software.
